---
title: "Model Documentation"
toc: true
format:
  html:
    include-after-body:
      text: |
        <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Share Your Feedback

Use the [Hypothesis](https://hypothes.is/) sidebar (click the `<` button on the right edge) to annotate any part of this documentation. Your comments help improve the model!
:::

## Overview

This document provides technical documentation for the cultured chicken cost model, including:

- Model structure and equations
- Parameter definitions and sources
- Methodology notes
- Limitations and caveats

## Model Structure

### Core Equation

The model estimates **unit production cost** ($/kg) as:

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \text{VOC} + \text{CAPEX}_{\text{annualized}} + \text{Fixed OPEX}
$$

Where:

- **VOC** (Variable Operating Costs) = Media + Micronutrients + Recombinant factors + Other
- **CAPEX** = Capital costs annualized via capital recovery factor (CRF)
- **Fixed OPEX** = Labor, maintenance, overhead, scaled with plant size

### Monte Carlo Simulation

The model uses Monte Carlo simulation (default: 30,000 samples) to propagate uncertainty:

1. Sample parameters from specified distributions
2. Apply technology adoption switches (Bernoulli draws)
3. Calculate unit cost for each sample
4. Report quantiles and probabilities

## Parameter Definitions

### Basic Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Distribution | Default | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------|---------|------|
| Plant capacity | $Q$ | Lognormal(p5, p95) | 5-50 | kTA/year |
| Utilization | $u$ | Beta(mean, sd) | 0.90 | fraction |
| Maturity index | $m$ | Beta(mean, sd) | 0.50 | 0-1 |

### Technology Adoption

Each technology has a base adoption probability, adjusted by maturity:

$$
P_{\text{adopted}} = \text{clip}(P_{\text{base}} + 0.25 \times (m - 0.5), 0, 1)
$$

| Technology | Base Probability | Effect |
|------------|-----------------|--------|
| Hydrolysates | 60% | Reduces media $/L by ~70% |
| Food-grade micros | 65% | Reduces micro $/g by ~90% |
| Cheap recombinant | 50% | Reduces growth factor cost dramatically |

### Process Intensities

| Parameter | Range (p5-p95) | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|------|-------|
| Cell density | 30-200 | g/L | Final biomass at harvest |
| Cycle time | 0.5-5 | days | Time per production batch |
| Media turnover | 1-10 | ratio | 1=batch, >1=perfusion |

### Cost Components

#### Variable Costs

| Component | Formula | Key Drivers |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Media | $L/kg \times \$/L$ | Density, turnover, hydrolysates |
| Comm. micros | $g/kg \times \$/g$ | Food-grade adoption |
| Recombinant | $g/kg \times \$/g$ | Cheap tech adoption |
| Other VOC | Lognormal(0.5, 5) | Utilities, consumables |

#### Capital Costs

Reactor CAPEX is computed bottom-up:

1. **Volume required**: $V = Q / (\text{productivity} \times 365)$
2. **Reactor cost**: Blend of pharma ($50-500/L) and custom reactors
3. **Scaling**: Apply power law $K \propto V^s$ where $s \in [0.6, 0.9]$
4. **Lang factor**: Multiply by 1.5-3.5x for total installed plant
5. **Annualize**: Apply CRF based on WACC and asset life

$$
\text{CAPEX}_{/kg} = \frac{\text{CAPEX}_{\text{total}} \times \text{CRF}}{Q \times u}
$$

#### Fixed OPEX

Scaled from reference plant (20 kTA @ 90% utilization):

$$
\text{Fixed OPEX} = \text{Ref} \times \left(\frac{Q}{Q_{\text{ref}}}\right)^s
$$

Where $s \in [0.6, 1.0]$ is the scaling exponent.

## Maturity Correlation

A key feature of this model is the **latent maturity factor** $m \in [0, 1]$ that induces positive correlation between:

- Technology adoption probabilities
- Custom reactor share
- WACC (lower in mature industry)

This reflects the intuition that in "good worlds" for cultured chicken:
- Technologies are more likely to be adopted
- Industry has more custom solutions
- Financing costs are lower due to reduced risk

## Limitations

### Known Limitations

1. **Static snapshot model**: The model projects costs for a single target year (adjustable 2026-2050). It does not model year-over-year learning curves, experience curves, or cost trajectories. Instead, the "maturity" parameter serves as a proxy for cumulative industry development. Lower maturity values represent scenarios where progress has been slower; higher values represent faster progress.

2. **Downstream processing is optional**: Texturization, scaffolding, and forming costs can be included via the "Include downstream processing" toggle. When enabled, this adds $2-15/kg for structured products. Not needed for ground meat applications.

3. **No geography**: Assumes generic global costs, not region-specific factors (labor rates, energy costs, regulatory environments).

4. **Limited contamination modeling**: No batch failure distributions or contamination event modeling. Real-world operations would face periodic batch losses.

5. **Some correlations may be underspecified**: While the maturity factor induces correlation between technology adoption, reactor costs, and WACC, other potential correlations (e.g., between cell density and cycle time) are treated as independent.

### Recommendations for Use

| Recommendation | Justification |
|----------------|---------------|
| Use for **relative comparisons** rather than absolute predictions | Model parameters have wide uncertainty bands; the direction and magnitude of differences between scenarios is more robust than point estimates |
| Focus on **probability thresholds** (P < $10/kg) rather than point estimates | Probabilities integrate over parameter uncertainty and are more decision-relevant for pivotal questions |
| **Validate key parameters** with domain experts before publication | Literature-derived ranges may not reflect latest industry knowledge; some parameters (e.g., growth factor costs) are changing rapidly |
| **Report uncertainty ranges**, not just medians | Wide 90% CIs reflect genuine epistemic uncertainty; hiding this gives false confidence |
| **Consider correlated scenarios** via the maturity slider | The maturity factor captures the intuition that "good worlds" have multiple improvements occurring together |

## Code Reference

The full model implementation is available in the repository:

- `dashboard/model.py` - Core simulation code
- `models/cm_cost_v0.2.squiggle` - Squiggle version

## Changelog

| Version | Date | Changes |
|---------|------|---------|
| v0.2 | 2026-02 | Explicit scale/uptime, beta distributions, maturity factor |
| v0.1 | 2026-01 | Initial Squiggle model based on UC Davis |
